common_name: Turk's Turban Squash (Turban Squash, French Turban, Giraumon Turban)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; Turk's Turban is grown as much for ornament as for eating — the extraordinary multi-colored fruits with their protruding turban-shaped secondary lobe are among the most visually dramatic of any squash, making them a centerpiece of autumn decorations and farmers' market displays as well as a rich-flavored eating squash.

directions: Direct sow in hills of 3 seeds (thin to 2) after last frost in a fertile, well-drained bed; Turk's Turban produces medium-length vines of 6–8 feet; harvest when the skin is fully hard and the color has developed to its mature orange-red with green and white swirling markings; cure at room temperature for 2–3 weeks to harden the rind and improve flavor. Turk's Turban is one of the most visually spectacular squashes in cultivation — an heirloom Cucurbita maxima variety that has been grown in American and European gardens since the 19th century, renowned for the extraordinary shape of its fruits: a squat, flattened orange-red primary body topped by a rounded, turban-like secondary lobe that protrudes from the blossom end, often striped or mottled in contrasting shades of green, cream, and orange. The distinctive shape comes from an unusual growth pattern in which the secondary floral section of the fruit expands outward from the blossom end, creating the appearance of a hat or turban imposed on top of the primary fruit body. No two Turk's Turban fruits are exactly alike in their color patterning — each shows a unique arrangement of orange, green, cream, and white in swirls and stripes that makes them natural art objects. The flesh beneath is the dense, sweet, orange type characteristic of the best Cucurbita maxima varieties; at full maturity after curing, the flavor is rich and sweet with a dry, starchy texture excellent for baking, soups, and puréeing. While widely grown as a purely ornamental squash for autumn display, Turk's Turban is fully edible and good-flavored — it deserves to be eaten rather than simply displayed.

common_name: Musquée de Provence Pumpkin (Cheese Pumpkin, Fairytale-Type French Heirloom)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Musquée de Provence requires a long, warm season (105–130 days) and is best suited to zones 5–11 with adequate summer heat; this is the classic large French market pumpkin traditionally sold in wheel-cut slices at French marché stalls — a deeply ribbed, tan to olive-green flattened pumpkin of 8–20 lbs with extraordinarily rich, sweet, deep-orange flesh that is considered among the finest flavored of all pumpkins.

directions: Direct sow in prepared hills after last frost; this is a large, long-season vine requiring 6–10 feet of space; mulch well to retain moisture; harvest when skin turns from green to a tan-olive-brown and the rind resists fingernail penetration; cure at room temperature 2–4 weeks to improve flavor and extend storage to 6+ months. Musquée de Provence is one of France's great culinary pumpkins — a Cucurbita moschata variety native to the Provence region of southern France where it is sold by the cut-slice in farmers' markets and street markets, displayed on the vendor's table as great wheels of orange-fleshed, aromatic squash. The name means 'musk-scented from Provence' — the ripe fruits emit a subtle, sweet, musky fragrance characteristic of fully mature Cucurbita moschata varieties, similar to the fragrance of a ripe butternut squash but more intense. The fruits are dramatically large (10–20 lbs), deeply ribbed like a pumpkin, and conspicuously flattened (more wide than tall), with a mature skin color of tan, buff, or olive-gray-green that turns warm amber-tan over the course of curing. The flesh inside is a uniform, very deep orange-yellow, dense, dry, and intensely sweet — widely considered among the most flavorful pumpkin varieties available, with a rich, complex sweetness that makes it superb for soups, puréed dishes, and French pumpkin gratin (gratin de potiron). Related to but distinct from the 'Fairytale' pumpkin (Cucurbita maxima), Musquée de Provence is a moschata type with corresponding hot-climate adaptability — it is more heat and humidity tolerant than most winter squash.

common_name: Sweet Meat Squash (Oregon Homestead Sweet Meat, Gray Hubbard-Type)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Sweet Meat is a large (8–15 lb) slate-blue to gray-green winter squash of the Cucurbita maxima species, historically important in the Pacific Northwest as a premier eating and storing squash; it is the variety that defined the 'gray Hubbard' category of dry, sweet, long-storing winter squash in American home gardens.

directions: Direct sow in hills after last frost in rich, well-drained soil; Sweet Meat requires a long vine (8–12 feet) and ample space; harvest when the skin is fully gray-blue and rock-hard; it stores exceptionally well (6–8 months in cool, dry conditions) and the flavor improves with several months of storage as starches convert to sugars. Sweet Meat squash is a prized heirloom of the Pacific Northwest, where it has been grown in Oregon and Washington for generations as the gold standard of winter keeping squash — valued above all other varieties for the combination of very long storage life, exceptional flavor, and high-quality flesh. The fruits are large (8–15 lbs), somewhat flattened round to oval, with a smooth, dry, slate-blue to gray-green skin that turns distinctly grayish-blue at full maturity — a color that is characteristic of the very best long-storing Cucurbita maxima varieties. The flesh is uniformly deep orange, very dry, dense, and extremely sweet — with a characteristic thick, starchy quality that makes it ideal for baking, puréeing, and any preparation where flavor concentration and dry texture are paramount. The famous Oregon home economist and food writer Carol Deppe championed Sweet Meat squash extensively in her book 'The Resilient Gardener' as the ideal squash for serious food gardeners seeking long storage life and superior flavor; her advocacy brought Sweet Meat to wider national attention. Unlike most winter squash that peak at harvest and slowly decline in storage, Sweet Meat actually improves for the first 2–3 months in storage as the chemistry continues to develop, making it the ideal squash for a Thanksgiving centerpiece planted in late spring.

common_name: Orangetti Spaghetti Squash (Orange Spaghetti Squash, Golden Spaghetti Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Orangetti is a compact, early-maturing spaghetti squash that matures to a vivid deep orange exterior (rather than the cream-white of standard spaghetti squash) with higher beta-carotene content and the same characteristic stranded flesh texture — it is earlier, more flavorful, and more nutritious than standard spaghetti squash.

directions: Direct sow after last frost; Orangetti is semi-compact with shorter vines than standard spaghetti squash; harvest when the outer skin is a uniform deep orange and the rind is firm; bake halved at 375°F for 40–50 minutes and scrape the interior with a fork to produce the characteristic pasta-like strands that give spaghetti squash its name. Orangetti is a distinctive type of spaghetti squash developed in Israel through breeding work at the Negev branch of the Agricultural Research Organization, selected for its vivid orange skin color (versus the pale cream-yellow of standard spaghetti squash), higher beta-carotene content in both the skin and the stranded flesh, and slightly earlier maturity than most spaghetti squash varieties. The vegetable has the same characteristic feature that makes all spaghetti squash unique — the interior flesh separates into long, pasta-like strands when cooked — but the Orangetti variant produces strands that are slightly yellower-orange than the pale yellow of standard varieties, reflecting the higher pigment content. The flavor of Orangetti is considered by many cooks to be sweeter and more complex than standard white spaghetti squash, though still with the mild, somewhat neutral quality that makes spaghetti squash a versatile pasta substitute for low-carbohydrate diets. The compact, semi-bush vine habit is more manageable than standard spaghetti squash in small gardens. Orangetti typically yields 3–5 fruits per vine weighing 2–4 lbs each.

common_name: Baby Blue Hubbard Squash (Little Gem Hubbard, Compact Blue Hubbard)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Baby Blue Hubbard is a compact, appropriately sized version of the giant Blue Hubbard squash, producing 4–8 lb teardrop-shaped, slate-blue fruits with the same exceptional sweet, dry, richly flavored flesh of the full-sized type — but in a manageable size that a typical household can consume in 2–3 sittings and a vine that requires much less space.

directions: Direct sow in hills after last frost; Baby Blue Hubbard produces compact vines of 4–6 feet, more manageable in home gardens than full-sized Hubbard; harvest when the teardrop-shaped fruits are fully blue-gray and the skin resists fingernail penetration; cure for 2–3 weeks and store up to 6 months in a cool, dry location. Baby Blue Hubbard is a compact selection from the classic Blue Hubbard squash (Cucurbita maxima 'Hubbard') — one of the premier American heirloom winter squash varieties, famous for its distinctive blue-gray, bumpy, teardrop-shaped fruits and its extraordinarily sweet, dry, richly flavored flesh. The standard Blue Hubbard can produce fruits of 15–25 lbs or more — too large for most modern households to use in one or even several meals, requiring the awkward step of cutting up a huge squash and storing the remainder. Baby Blue Hubbard addresses this practical limitation by producing fruits of 4–8 lbs with the same culinary qualities as the full-sized type: the thick, deeply orange, dry, intensely sweet flesh that has made Hubbard squash the gold standard of American winter squash for over a century. The skin is the same distinctive blue-gray, somewhat bumpy, teardrop-shaped form of the parent variety, recognizable at a glance, and equally hard to cut open (a mallet or drop from a height is the traditional method). The flavor after curing is superb — often described as the richest, most complex flavor of any winter squash, combining sweetness with an earthy, slightly nutty character. Stores exceptionally well for 4–6 months.
